candidate_sources.title: Bacterial blight on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial blight is caused by Pseudomonas syringae pv. glycinea. Small, angular, translucent, water-soaked, yellow to light brown lesions are often found on soybean leaves, but may also be found on soybean stems, petioles, and pods. As lesions age they turn yellow, then reddish-brown, then black, leaving a yellowish-green halo. The bacteria overwinters in crop residues. Cool, wet weather (70-80°F) favors infection. Manage with resistant varieties, pathogen-free seed, and crop rotation.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Blight of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial blight of soybean, caused by Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. glycinea, is the most common bacterial disease of soybean. Symptoms include small angular water-soaked lesions surrounded by yellow-green halos that coalesce into dark-brown necrotic areas. The disease is favored by cool (70 to 80 °F), wet weather and inhibited by hot, dry weather. Management includes resistant cultivars, pathogen-free seed, and crop rotation.

candidate_sources.title: Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. glycinea (bacterial blight of soybean) | CABI Compendium
candidate_sources.snippet: CABI datasheet on Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. glycinea, causal agent of bacterial blight of soybean. Symptoms are conspicuous on leaves and consist of small angular lesions, usually water-soaked at the centre and surrounded by a yellow-green halo. Later, the spots coalesce to form dark-brown necrotic areas with yellow margins. The pathogen overwinters in crop residues and can be seed transmitted; inoculum is splashed onto healthy tissues by wind and rainfall.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Blight - Extended Version | OSU Soybean Diseases
candidate_sources.snippet: Ohio State University extended factsheet on bacterial blight of soybean caused by Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. glycinea. Covers symptoms (angular water-soaked lesions with yellow halos), disease cycle (overwintering in residue, seed transmission, splash dispersal), favorable conditions (cool wet weather), and management (resistant varieties, clean seed, rotation, tillage of residue).

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial pustule on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Symptoms begin as small, light green spots (not water-soaked) with raised centers on the upper and lower surfaces of leaves. Light-colored pustules (blisters) often develop in the center of lesions, especially on the undersides of leaves. Caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines. Develops in warm (85-90°F), moist conditions.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Pustule | CropWatch - University of Nebraska–Lincoln
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial pustule on soybean caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines. Initial infection results in tiny pale green spots on new leaves with raised centers; small light colored pustules form in the center of spots, more common on lower leaf surfaces.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial pustule - Cornell CALS
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial pustule of soybean caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines. Pathogen overwinters in unburied crop residue and on seeds; transmitted by seed and spread by windblown rain, rain splash, and machinery during wet conditions.

candidate_sources.title: Chapter 58: Bacterial Diseases of Soybeans - SDSU Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: SDSU Extension chapter covering bacterial diseases of soybeans including bacterial pustule (Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines), bacterial blight, and wildfire — symptoms, disease cycle, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines (soyabean bacterial pustule) - CABI Plantwise Knowledge Bank
candidate_sources.snippet: CABI datasheet on Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines, the causal agent of soybean bacterial pustule — distribution, host range, symptoms, biology, and control.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Pustule Disease in Soybean: Occurrence, Host–Pathogen Interactions and Management - Wang 2025 - Plant Pathology
candidate_sources.snippet: Peer-reviewed review of bacterial pustule (BP) caused by Xanthomonas citri pv. glycines (X. axonopodis pv. glycines), yield reductions up to 60% under favorable conditions; covers host-pathogen interactions and management including oxytetracycline efficacy >70%.

candidate_sources.title: Bean pod mottle virus on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Bean pod mottle virus causes green to yellow mottling of young leaves, and in severe infections, leaves may become distorted. BPMV is transmitted by leaf-feeding beetles, specifically the bean leaf beetle (Cerotoma trifurcata). Yield losses as high as 52% and reduced seed quality.

candidate_sources.title: Bean Pod Mottle Virus | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Symptoms associated with BPMV include mild to severe chlorotic mottling or mosaic and rugosity (distortion or wrinkling) on foliage and pods, stunting, and delayed maturity. BPMV is a member of the genus Comovirus in the family Comoviridae, comprised by a bipartite, positive, single-stranded RNA encapsidated in isometric particles of 28 nm.

candidate_sources.title: Bean Pod Mottle Virus | CropWatch | Nebraska
candidate_sources.snippet: Symptoms of BPMV include green to yellow mottling of younger leaves in the upper canopy. The virus is transmitted most commonly by the bean leaf beetle in soybean. Insecticide seed treatments and foliar sprays targeting management of the bean leaf beetle will reduce the incidence of BPMV. Currently, there are no BPMV resistant soybean varieties commercially available.

candidate_sources.title: Bean pod mottle virus: A Threat to U.S. Soybean Production
candidate_sources.snippet: Peer-reviewed paper on Bean pod mottle virus as a threat to U.S. soybean production, covering virus biology, transmission by bean leaf beetle, epidemiology, and management strategies.

candidate_sources.title: Cercospora Leaf Blight of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: The first symptom of Cercospora leaf blight is the development of light purple spots and areas on the top surface of leaves exposed to light, which expand and deepen in color to a reddish-purple or bronze. Diseased leaves will yellow and can have a bronze or purple tint. Caused by Cercospora kikuchii.

candidate_sources.title: Cercospora leaf blight and purple seed stain on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Cercospora leaf blight is a foliar disease caused by the pathogen Cercospora kikuchii, the causal agent of purple seed stain. In high humidity and heat, C. kikuchii spores germinate and infect susceptible soybean plants, producing the toxin cercosporin which causes purple-bronze discoloration.

candidate_sources.title: Overview of Cercospora Leaf Blight of Soybean | Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Comprehensive overview of Cercospora leaf blight including symptoms, pathogen biology (C. kikuchii and C. flagellaris), disease cycle, and integrated management. C. flagellaris has been reported to be the dominant species across the southern U.S. soybean belt.

candidate_sources.title: Cercospora Leaf Blight and Purple Seed Stain of Soybean | NDSU Agriculture
candidate_sources.snippet: NDSU Extension factsheet covering symptoms on leaves and seed (pink to purple discoloration extending from the hilum), favorable conditions (humid, 75-80 F), and management including rotation to corn/small grains, tillage, seed treatment, and foliar fungicides at R3-R5.

candidate_sources.title: An Overview of Charcoal Rot of Soybean
candidate_sources.snippet: Charcoal rot of soybean is caused by the soilborne fungus Macrophomina phaseolina, which can infect around 100 agricultural crop and weed species. Symptoms include smaller than normal leaves, reduced vigor, premature yellowing of top leaves, plants wilting during the midday heat, and black dusty microsclerotia in stem tissues.

candidate_sources.title: Charcoal rot on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Charcoal rot is caused by Macrophomina phaseolina. Roots and stems near the soil surface of infected plants will appear gray with tiny black specks or dots (resembling charcoal dust). Plants may be stunted, wilted, bearing abnormally small leaves that turn chlorotic and necrotic but remain attached to the plant. Hot, dry conditions favor symptom development.

candidate_sources.title: Soybean Charcoal Rot | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Charcoal rot of soybean is caused by Macrophomina phaseolina, an Ascomycete fungus. At seedling stage typical symptoms include brown to dark spots on cotyledons; at the unifoliate leaf stage circular to oblong, reddish-brown lesions that turn dark brown to black are observed. Mid to late season symptoms include light brown to grey superficial lesions in the stem with microsclerotia visible as black specks.

candidate_sources.title: Soybean Disease Management: Charcoal Rot (CPN-1004)
candidate_sources.snippet: Crop Protection Network publication on charcoal rot of soybean caused by Macrophomina phaseolina. Discusses symptoms (microsclerotia in vascular tissue, wilting, stunted plants), disease cycle, hot/dry favorable conditions, and management practices including rotation with small grains, no-till, and full-season variety selection.

candidate_sources.title: Downy mildew on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Initial symptoms of downy mildew are small, light green spots on upper leaf surfaces, which enlarge and turn pale to bright yellow before potentially coalescing into large irregular brown areas. Tan to gray tufts of fungal growth often develop on lower leaf surfaces. Caused by Peronospora manshurica, favored by high humidity and moderate temperatures.

candidate_sources.title: Downy Mildew of Soybean - Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Downy mildew is caused by the fungal-like organism Peronospora manshurica. The pathogen is in the same group of water mold pathogens that cause Phytophthora root rot and Pythium seedling blight of soybean. Has swimming spores and needs water or high humidity for infection. Disease rarely reduces yield; fungicides not recommended.

candidate_sources.title: Downy Mildew - Soybean Disease - Soybean Research & Information Network (SRIN)
candidate_sources.snippet: Downy mildew of soybean caused by Peronospora manshurica. Symptoms include light green spots on upper leaf surfaces enlarging to yellow areas, with tan to gray fungal tufts on lower leaf surfaces. Infected seeds become covered with whitish coating of fungal hyphae and spores. Management includes resistant varieties, residue burial, and crop rotation.

candidate_sources.title: Downy mildew - Soybeans - Cornell CALS
candidate_sources.snippet: Downy mildew on soybeans caused by Peronospora manshurica. Dew and high humidity favor disease development, as well as mild temperatures. Can occur on plants of all ages, though most common after flowering begins, with young leaves most susceptible.

candidate_sources.title: Frogeye Leaf Spot of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Early FLS symptoms are small, dark, water soaked, circular to angular lesions (generally 1-5 mm diameter). Spots eventually enlarge to a diameter of about 1/4 inch, and the centers of the lesions become gray to brown and have a reddish-purple margin. C. sojina may infect stems, causing dark red to brown lesions. On pods, C. sojina causes circular to oblong, reddish, sunken lesions.

candidate_sources.title: Frogeye Leaf Spot of Soybean - Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Comprehensive Crop Protection Network encyclopedia entry on frogeye leaf spot caused by Cercospora sojina, including symptoms, disease cycle, conditions favoring disease, and management practices including resistant cultivars, crop rotation, tillage, and foliar fungicides. QoI fungicide resistance has been reported since 2010 across more than 20 U.S. soybean-producing states.

candidate_sources.title: Frogeye Leaf Spot - Purdue Extension BP-131-W
candidate_sources.snippet: Purdue Extension factsheet on Frogeye Leaf Spot of soybean caused by Cercospora sojina. Discusses symptoms on leaves, stems, pods, and seeds; conditions favoring disease (warm, humid weather with frequent rainfall); disease cycle (overwinters in residue and seed); and management recommendations.

candidate_sources.title: Frogeye leaf spot caused by Cercospora sojina: A review | Tropical Plant Pathology
candidate_sources.snippet: Peer-reviewed review of frogeye leaf spot caused by Cercospora sojina, covering pathogen biology, epidemiology, symptoms, host range, race structure, fungicide resistance (QoI), and integrated management strategies for soybean.

candidate_sources.title: Phytophthora root and stem rot on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Phytophthora root and stem rot of soybean is caused by Phytophthora sojae, which is not a true fungus, but belongs to a group of fungal-like organisms called oomycetes (water molds). Seedlings can be attacked and killed in the ground or soon after emergence. Infected stems appear bruised and are soft, secondary roots are rotted, the leaves turn yellow and brown and plants can wilt and die.

candidate_sources.title: Phytopthora Root and Stem Rot of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Phytophthora root and stem rot of soybean caused by Phytophthora sojae. The roots and lower stem turn a dark brown color, the leaves of the plant turn yellow and necrotic. The wilted leaves remain attached to the plant. The disease often occurs after wet weather, especially in low, poorly drained areas where saturated soil occurred early in the season.

candidate_sources.title: Phytophthora Root and Stem Rot of Soybean | Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Phytophthora root and stem rot is a soil-borne fungal-like disease caused by Phytophthora sojae that causes seed rots, pre- and post-emergence damping off of seedlings and stem rot of plants at various growth stages. A characteristic of P. sojae infections is the dark chocolate brown discoloration extending from the base of the plant up the stem.

candidate_sources.title: Rhizoctonia root and stem rot on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Rhizoctonia root and stem rot of soybean caused by Rhizoctonia solani. Symptoms include reddish-brown lesions on stems and roots, damping off of seedlings, and stunted plants. Disease favored by warm, moist soil conditions.

candidate_sources.title: Sclerotinia Stem Rot (White Mold) of Soybean | Ohioline
candidate_sources.snippet: Sclerotinia stem rot of soybean is caused by the fungus Sclerotinia sclerotiorum. This pathogen has a very wide host range with more than 400 plant species. Diseased plants first appear wilted with grayish green leaves that later turn brown to tan; stems show grayish to bleached white lesions covered by white fluffy mycelia under high moisture. Sclerotia (black, hard, irregular survival structures) form inside or outside stems and pods.

candidate_sources.title: Sclerotinia stem rot (white mold) on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: White mold (also named Sclerotinia stem rot) is a common and destructive disease in northern U.S. and Canada caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum. Lesions develop first at stem nodes during or after flowering; tops become grayish-green, wilt and die. Infected stems become soft and watery and covered with white moldy growth; eventually bleached and stringy. Sclerotia survive in soil; apothecia produce ascospores that infect dead flowers under cool, wet conditions.

candidate_sources.title: White Mold of Soybean | Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: White mold of soybean caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum. Symptoms include water-soaked stem lesions that progress above and below infected nodes and eventually encircle the stem; infected stems become bleached and stringy with white cottony mycelia and black sclerotia. Management includes resistant varieties, row spacing/population adjustments, rotation with corn or small grains, irrigation management, and foliar fungicides at flowering.

candidate_sources.title: White mold - Purdue Extension (BP-43-W)
candidate_sources.snippet: Purdue Extension factsheet on white mold (Sclerotinia stem rot) of soybean caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum. Describes symptoms (bleached stem lesions, white fluffy mycelium, black sclerotia), disease cycle (sclerotia germinate to form apothecia, ascospores infect senescing flowers), and integrated management.

candidate_sources.title: Southern Blight of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Comprehensive factsheet on Southern Blight of soybean caused by Sclerotium rolfsii, prepared by the NC State University Field Crops and Tobacco Pathology Lab. Covers symptoms (yellowing, wilting, dark brown stem lesions at soil line, white fan-like mycelium and tan-brown sclerotia), pathogen survival, conditions favoring disease, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Southern Blight of Soybeans - Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Encyclopedia entry on Southern Blight of soybeans. Describes seedling pre/post-emergence damping-off, yellowing and wilting of older plants, brown girdling lesions at the soil surface, white moldy growth, and sclerotia. Discusses sclerotia overwintering and high temperature (77-95F) and moist conditions favoring disease, plus management with crop rotation to non-host crops (maize, sorghum, wheat).

candidate_sources.title: Southern Blight/Sclerotium Blight - UT Crops Pest Guides
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Tennessee soybean pest guide entry on Southern Blight / Sclerotium Blight caused by Sclerotium rolfsii. Provides identification of stem lesions and white mycelial mat with mustard-seed-sized sclerotia, conditions favoring disease, and management recommendations for soybean.

candidate_sources.title: Southern blight, southern stem blight, white mold - APS Education Center
candidate_sources.snippet: American Phytopathological Society (APS) Plant Disease Lesson on Southern blight (also called southern stem blight or white mold) caused by Sclerotium rolfsii / Athelia rolfsii. Covers wide host range (>200 species including soybean), pathogen biology, symptoms, signs (white mycelium, tan-brown sclerotia), and disease cycle.

candidate_sources.title: Asian Soybean Rust | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: At the beginning of the disease, small, tan-colored lesions, restricted by leaf veins, can be observed on infected soybean leaves. Lesions enlarge and, 5-8 days after initial infection, rust pustules (uredia) become visible. Uredia develop more frequently in lesions on the lower surface of the leaf than on the upper surface. Caused by Phakopsora pachyrhizi.

candidate_sources.title: Soybean rust | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Soybean rust caused by Phakopsora pachyrhizi. Symptoms include gray-green, tan to dark-brown, or reddish-brown lesions that may become erumpant on leaflets. Rust epidemics are most severe during long periods of rain and temperatures between 60-82F (16-28C). Fungicide applications made between R3 and R5 may provide the most yield protection.

candidate_sources.title: Soybean Rust | Ohioline - The Ohio State University
candidate_sources.snippet: Soybean rust is caused by the fungus Phakopsora pachyrhizi. Under favorable conditions, the pathogen can cause yield losses greater than 50 percent. Lesions tend to be angular with 2-5 mm diameter, and can affect not only leaves, but may be observed on petioles, pods, and stems. The primary management of rust is through the use of fungicide applications.
